grammar.usage: Used with singular countable nouns when referring to something non-specific or mentioned for the first time.
Used with singular/plural countable nouns and uncountable nouns when referring to something specific or unique.
grammar.example: The Alps are a beautiful mountain range.

grammar.usage: Countable nouns can be singular or plural. Uncountable nouns only have a singular form.
grammar.example: Some important news has just come in.

grammar.usage: Used to express quantity (e.g. many, much, a lot of, a few, a little).
grammar.example: How many channels do you get?

rows:
* Used with singular countable nouns:
  - Non-specific things
  - Mentioned for the first time

* Use 'an' with words starting with vowels (u, e, o, a, i)
* Note: Use 'a' or 'an' based on pronunciation, not spelling
  - EX: a university /juːnɪˈvɜːsəti/
  - EX: an hour /aʊər/

* Used with singular countable nouns:
  - Specific things
  - Generalizations about a class of things

* Used with plural countable nouns
* Used with uncountable nouns (e.g., rice, hair, oil, gasoline...)
* Used with unique things:
  - EX: the Earth, the Moon, the World Cup, the Miss World...
* Note: Definite article pronunciation changes depending on the following word

bullets: Do not use articles with plural countable nouns or uncountable nouns when speaking generally.
EX1: Journalists often face dangerous situations. (countable & general)
EX2: News travels fast these days. (uncountable & general)

richGrammar.title: B) ARTICLE USAGE IN SPECIAL CASES

rows: in the 1990s, in (the) summer, in the morning
have a job, work as a waiter...
the Queen, the Principal, the French...
become president, go to work, be at work, have work to do, he's French
The Alps, the River Thames, the Arctic...
the media, on the radio, play the guitar...
listen to music, on television, play tennis...
The army, the police, the fire brigade
go to the school (as a visitor), be in the first year
go to school (as a student), be in Class 8B
take a taxi, catch a bus...
catch the bus, on the bus...
on foot, go home, go by bus...
the bank, the tax office, go to the hospital (as a visitor)
go to hospital (as a nurse, doctor...)

advice, information, knowledge, news, coffee, water, glass, hair, rice, oil, gas, paper, furniture, luggage, money, homework, work...

Plural-only nouns (take plural verbs):
clothes, scissors, jeans, spectacles, trousers, groceries...

Singular or plural
EX: How many channels do you get?

Singular (except plural-only nouns)
EX1: Some important news has just come in.
EX2: Your advice was very useful.
Used with countable nouns (mainly in negative sentences & questions)
Used with uncountable nouns (mainly in negative sentences & questions)
Used with both countable and uncountable nouns (mainly in affirmative sentences)
Used with countable nouns (means 'not many' - almost none)
Used with uncountable nouns (means 'not much' - almost none)

vocabulary.meaning: a rare and exceptionally good chance to do something
vocabulary.example: This scholarship is a unique opportunity to study abroad.

vocabulary.meaning: the sea surrounded by Europe, Asia, and Africa
vocabulary.example: They went on a cruise around the Mediterranean.

vocabulary.meaning: a spaceflight or expedition that has a human crew on board
vocabulary.example: NASA is planning a manned mission to Mars.

vocabulary.meaning: an event causing great suffering, destruction, and distress
vocabulary.example: The car crash was a terrible tragedy.

vocabulary.meaning: a plan of action designed to achieve a long-term or overall aim
vocabulary.example: The company developed a new marketing strategy.

vocabulary.meaning: not guilty of a crime or offense
vocabulary.example: The jury found him innocent of all charges.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who has personally seen something happen
vocabulary.example: The police are interviewing eye-witnesses of the accident.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who attacks someone in order to steal their money
vocabulary.example: The mugger ran away with her handbag.

vocabulary.meaning: to scatter small drops or particles, or a light shower of rain
vocabulary.example: He sprinkled some sugar on the strawberries.

vocabulary.meaning: a regular election of candidates for office, e.g., for parliament
vocabulary.example: The prime minister called a general election for next month.

vocabulary.meaning: activities associated with the governance of a country or area
vocabulary.example: She decided to enter politics after university.

vocabulary.meaning: the head of an elected government
vocabulary.example: The Prime Minister gave a speech outside Downing Street.

vocabulary.meaning: to approve and enact a new piece of legislation
vocabulary.example: The parliament passed a law to ban plastic bags.

vocabulary.meaning: to completely change the way something is done

vocabulary.example: Water is vital for all living things.

vocabulary.meaning: the formal decision made by a jury in a court trial
vocabulary.example: We are waiting for the jury's verdict.
